content.text: Why Barrier Diagnosis Precedes Intervention

The most common programme mistake is applying the same intervention to all non-adopters regardless of why they are not adopting. Running more lunch-and-learn sessions for users who have accounts but cannot access them (an access barrier) wastes champion time and erodes confidence in the programme. Sending awareness emails to users who have already tried Claude and found it irrelevant (a relevance barrier) accelerates their disengagement.
Adoption barriers fall into four root-cause categories that each require a different response. Accurate diagnosis — before any intervention — is the highest-leverage action a programme team can take.

attrs.alt: Adoption barrier taxonomy — four root cause categories with signals and fixes

content.text: The Four Barrier Categories
Category
Root Cause
Funnel Symptom
Misdiagnosis Risk
Access
User cannot physically reach Claude — no account, blocked by IT, or approval lag
Low Aware → Activated conversion despite high awareness
Often mistaken for awareness or confidence; check provisioning before any content intervention
Awareness
User does not know Claude exists, what it does, or that it applies to their role
Low Aware count relative to target population; comms reach gap
Often mistaken for relevance; users who haven't heard of Claude cannot evaluate its fit
Confidence
User knows about Claude and has access but is reluctant to start — fear of looking foolish, accuracy concerns, job anxiety
High activation rate but very low session depth on first use; high Day-1 dropout
Often mistaken for relevance; the user actually has relevant use cases but lacks the confidence to attempt them
Relevance
User has access and confidence but has tried Claude and found it did not help their specific work
High activation, low Week-2 return after a first session
Often mistaken for confidence; the user did try it — the gap is task-fit, not willingness

Always rule out access barriers first. They are invisible in survey data (users with no account cannot complete a survey about why they haven't logged in) but show up clearly in provisioning audits. A 3-minute provisioning audit can save months of misdirected content investment.
Diagnosing Barriers with Claude
Triangulating funnel data, access audit results, and survey responses gives a robust barrier diagnosis. Claude can synthesise these three inputs and recommend prioritised interventions:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def diagnose_adoption_barriers(
    department: str,
    funnel_data: dict,
    survey_responses: list[str],
    access_audit: dict
) -> dict:
    """Diagnose the primary adoption barriers for a department using multiple data sources."""
    # Calculate conversion rates
    aware = funnel_data.get("aware", 0)
    activated = funnel_data.get("activated", 0)
    regular = funnel_data.get("regular", 0)

    aware_to_activated = round(activated / max(aware, 1) * 100, 1)
    activated_to_regular = round(regular / max(activated, 1) * 100, 1)

    survey_text = "\n".join(f"- {r}" for r in survey_responses[:10])

    prompt = f"""Diagnose the primary adoption barriers for a Claude deployment in one department.

Department: {department}

Funnel performance:
- Aware → Activated: {aware_to_activated}% (benchmark: 55-65%)
- Activated → Regular: {activated_to_regular}% (benchmark: 30-40%)

Access audit findings:
- Users with active accounts: {access_audit.get("accounts_provisioned", 0)} of {aware}
- Users who hit an access error in the past 30 days: {access_audit.get("access_errors", 0)}
- Average provisioning delay (request to access): {access_audit.get("avg_provisioning_days", 0)} days

Survey responses (why haven't you used Claude more / at all?):
{survey_text}

Classify the primary barriers and return JSON:
- primary_barrier_category: "access" | "awareness" | "confidence" | "relevance"
- secondary_barrier_category: same options or null
- evidence: 2-3 specific data points that support the diagnosis
- misdiagnosis_risk: what barrier might the programme WRONGLY focus on based on surface signals
- recommended_interventions: list of 3 interventions in priority order, each with:
  - name: string
  - description: string
  - expected_impact: "high" | "medium" | "low"
  - time_to_implement: "1 week" | "2-4 weeks" | "1-3 months"
- success_metric: how to know the intervention worked (specific, measurable)"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

diagnosis = diagnose_adoption_barriers(
    department="Legal",
    funnel_data={"aware": 90, "activated": 28, "regular": 6},
    survey_responses=[
        "I wasn't sure if I'm allowed to use it for client matters",
        "Tried it once but it didn't know our template format",
        "No one showed me how to use it for legal drafting specifically",
        "The outputs need so much editing I'm not sure it saves time",
        "I thought it was more for the tech teams",
        "Haven't had a clear use case explained to me"
    ],
    access_audit={
        "accounts_provisioned": 85,
        "access_errors": 3,
        "avg_provisioning_days": 1.2
    }
)
print(f"Primary barrier: {diagnosis['primary_barrier_category'].upper()}")
print(f"Secondary barrier: {diagnosis['secondary_barrier_category']}")
print(f"Misdiagnosis risk: {diagnosis['misdiagnosis_risk']}")
print("\nTop intervention:")
top = diagnosis["recommended_interventions"][0]
print(f"  {top['name']}: {top['description']} ({top['expected_impact']} impact, {top['time_to_implement']})")
print(f"Success metric: {diagnosis['success_metric']}")
Designing Intervention Plans

Once the primary barrier is identified, a structured intervention plan maps activities, resources, and milestones to a specific timeline. A clear hypothesis — stating what metric should change by how much — makes it possible to evaluate whether the intervention worked:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def design_intervention_plan(
    department: str,
    barrier_type: str,
    affected_user_count: int,
    available_resources: dict,
    timeline_weeks: int
) -> dict:
    """Design a structured intervention plan for a specific adoption barrier."""
    resources_text = "\n".join(f"- {k}: {v}" for k, v in available_resources.items())
    prompt = f"""Design a {timeline_weeks}-week intervention plan to address a "{barrier_type}" adoption barrier.

Target department: {department}
Affected users: {affected_user_count}
Available resources:
{resources_text}

Design a week-by-week intervention plan that:
1. Addresses the root cause, not just the symptom
2. Uses the available resources efficiently
3. Builds sustainable change, not a one-time spike
4. Has measurable milestones each week

Return JSON with:
- intervention_name: a clear, outcome-focused title (e.g. "Legal Department Claude Confidence Sprint")
- hypothesis: if-then statement of what you expect to change (e.g. "If we provide role-specific prompts + 1 guided session, activation-to-regular conversion will rise from 21% to 35% in 4 weeks")
- weekly_plan: list of {{week: int, focus: str, activities: list[str], milestone: str}}
- resource_allocation: how to use each resource across the plan
- go_no_go_criteria: what would cause you to abandon or pivot the intervention after Week 2
- expected_outcome: specific metric change expected by week {timeline_weeks}"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

plan = design_intervention_plan(
    department="Finance",
    barrier_type="relevance",
    affected_user_count=62,
    available_resources={
        "champion": "1 senior finance champion (4h/week available)",
        "content": "Existing prompt library (no finance-specific prompts yet)",
        "manager_support": "CFO has endorsed the programme verbally",
        "training_budget": "£500 for a catered lunch-and-learn session"
    },
    timeline_weeks=4
)
print(f"Plan: {plan['intervention_name']}")
print(f"Hypothesis: {plan['hypothesis']}")
print(f"Expected outcome: {plan['expected_outcome']}")
print("\nWeek-by-week:")
for week in plan["weekly_plan"]:
    print(f"  Week {week['week']}: {week['focus']} → {week['milestone']}")
Barrier Survey Design

Surveys are the primary data source for confidence and relevance barriers, which do not show up clearly in usage logs. A well-designed survey diagnoses root cause and routes different respondents (never-tried vs tried-and-stopped) to different questions:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_barrier_survey(
    department: str,
    stage_in_funnel: str,
    known_context: str
) -> dict:
    """Generate a targeted survey to diagnose adoption barriers at a specific funnel stage."""
    prompt = f"""Design a short barrier diagnostic survey for Claude non-adopters in one department.

Department: {department}
Funnel stage of respondents: {stage_in_funnel}
Known context: {known_context}

The survey should:
- Take ≤3 minutes to complete
- Surface the root cause category (access, awareness, confidence, or relevance)
- Use a mix of multiple-choice (for quantification) and one open-text (for nuance)
- Avoid leading questions that telegraph the "correct" answer
- Include a routing question so people who haven't tried Claude at all get different questions

Return JSON with:
- survey_title: string
- introduction: 1-sentence framing for respondents
- questions: list of {{
    id: str,
    text: str,
    type: "single_choice" | "multiple_choice" | "likert_5" | "open_text",
    options: list[str] or null,
    routing: {{if_answer: str, go_to: str}} or null
  }}
- analysis_guide: how to map responses to barrier categories"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

survey = generate_barrier_survey(
    department="HR",
    stage_in_funnel="Aware but not yet Activated",
    known_context="HR team of 35, all have accounts provisioned, comms sent 3 weeks ago, 0% activation"
)
print(f"Survey: {survey['survey_title']}")
print(f"Intro: {survey['introduction']}")
print(f"\n{len(survey['questions'])} questions:")
for q in survey["questions"][:3]:
    print(f"  [{q['type']}] {q['text']}")
    if q.get("options"):
        for opt in q["options"][:3]:
            print(f"    • {opt}")
print(f"\nAnalysis guide: {survey['analysis_guide']}")
Intervention Playbook by Barrier Type
Barrier
Tier-1 Intervention
Tier-2 Intervention
Avoid
Access
Provisioning audit + fast-track approval for flagged users (target: 48-hour SLA)
Manager-led access request bundled with team onboarding
Awareness campaigns before access is confirmed — creates demand you cannot fulfill
Awareness
Champion-led lunch-and-learn with role-specific examples
Manager briefing with use-case one-pager; CFO/VP endorsement email
Generic all-hands mentions without follow-up call-to-action and access link
Confidence
Guided first-session with a champion (15 min 1:1); safe-to-fail prompt templates
Peer buddy pairing; 'bad prompt' normalisation session showing iteration
Overwhelming first-timers with advanced features; skipping the 'here's how to start' step
Relevance
Role-specific use-case playbook (before/after workflow); tailored prompt library entries
Shadowing a colleague from same function who is already an embedded user
Re-running the same generic demo that failed to resonate; adding more features without diagnosing task-fit gap
Tracking Intervention Effectiveness
Set a clear pre-intervention baseline
— record the specific metric (e.g. activated-to-regular conversion for the Legal team) before the intervention starts. Without a baseline, 'improvement' is unfalsifiable.
Define a go/no-go criterion at Week 2

— interventions that show no movement in the leading indicator by mid-point should be pivoted or stopped. Persistence with a failing intervention is the second most common programme mistake after misdiagnosis.
Use a control group where possible

— if two comparable departments have the same barrier, apply the intervention in one and measure the difference. Even an informal comparison is more informative than a before/after in a single cohort.
Distinguish intervention effect from time effect
— adoption naturally increases over time as word spreads. Use the control group or prior-cohort trends to separate programme-driven improvement from organic growth.
Prioritising Across Multiple Departments

Most enterprise programmes face simultaneous adoption barriers across different departments, each needing a different intervention, with a single programme team that cannot serve everyone at once. Systematic prioritisation — based on users unblocked per unit of capacity — prevents the common trap of focusing on the most vocal department rather than the highest-leverage one:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def prioritise_multi_barrier_interventions(
    departments: list[dict],
    programme_capacity: dict
) -> dict:
    """Prioritise barrier interventions across multiple departments given limited capacity."""
    dept_text = json.dumps(departments, indent=2)
    capacity_text = json.dumps(programme_capacity, indent=2)

    prompt = f"""You are helping a Claude adoption programme team prioritise interventions
across multiple departments with limited capacity.

Department barrier assessments:
{dept_text}

Available programme capacity this quarter:
{capacity_text}

Design an optimal intervention portfolio that:
1. Addresses the highest-impact barriers first (most users unblocked per unit of effort)
2. Does not overcommit the available capacity
3. Sequences interventions so early wins build momentum for later ones
4. Accounts for dependencies (e.g. you can't run awareness sessions if access isn't provisioned)

Return JSON with:
- recommended_portfolio: list of {{department, barrier_type, intervention, capacity_cost, expected_users_unblocked, week_to_start}}
- excluded_interventions: list of {{department, reason_excluded}}
- sequencing_rationale: 2-sentence explanation of the sequencing logic
- total_capacity_used: summary of how much of each capacity type is consumed
- expected_aggregate_impact: what the portfolio is expected to change in total activated/regular user counts"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=900,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

portfolio = prioritise_multi_barrier_interventions(
    departments=[
        {"name": "Legal", "primary_barrier": "confidence", "affected_users": 45,
         "current_activation_rate": 0.18, "access_confirmed": True},
        {"name": "Finance", "primary_barrier": "relevance", "affected_users": 62,
         "current_activation_rate": 0.31, "access_confirmed": True},
        {"name": "Procurement", "primary_barrier": "access", "affected_users": 28,
         "current_activation_rate": 0.05, "access_confirmed": False},
        {"name": "Marketing", "primary_barrier": "awareness", "affected_users": 80,
         "current_activation_rate": 0.12, "access_confirmed": True},
    ],
    programme_capacity={
        "champion_hours_per_week": 8,
        "content_creation_days": 5,
        "it_provisioning_slots": 2,
        "budget_gbp": 1200,
        "weeks_in_quarter": 12
    }
)
print(f"Portfolio size: {len(portfolio['recommended_portfolio'])} interventions")
print(f"Sequencing: {portfolio['sequencing_rationale']}")
print(f"Expected aggregate impact: {portfolio['expected_aggregate_impact']}")
for item in portfolio["recommended_portfolio"]:
    print(f"  Week {item['week_to_start']}: {item['department']} ({item['barrier_type']}) — {item['intervention']}")
The Manager's Role in Barrier Removal

Manager attitude is the single variable most correlated with team-level adoption. A manager who has not endorsed Claude, does not model its use, and does not protect time for their team to experiment is a barrier in themselves — independent of the access, awareness, confidence, or relevance categorisation. This is not a classification failure: manager-as-barrier is a special case of the awareness category, where the awareness gap sits with the manager rather than the team members.
Personalised manager briefs — explaining their specific team's barrier and making one concrete ask — are more effective than general manager communications:

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def generate_manager_barrier_brief(
    manager_name: str,
    team_name: str,
    barrier_diagnosis: dict,
    team_metrics: dict
) -> str:
    """Generate a manager-specific brief explaining their team's adoption barrier and ask."""
    prompt = f"""Write a brief, actionable email to a manager explaining their team's Claude adoption barrier
and making a specific ask of them.

Manager: {manager_name}
Team: {team_name}
Team size: {team_metrics.get("team_size", "unknown")}
Current activation rate: {team_metrics.get("activation_rate", 0)*100:.0f}%
Primary barrier: {barrier_diagnosis.get("primary_barrier_category", "unknown")}
Barrier rationale: {barrier_diagnosis.get("evidence", [])}

The email should:
- Lead with a positive signal about the team (not just problems)
- Explain the barrier diagnosis in plain language, without jargon
- Make ONE specific ask of the manager (not a list of five things)
- Give them an out: acknowledge their time constraints
- Include a specific metric to watch in the next 4 weeks
- Be under 200 words
- Sign off from the Claude programme team, not from an AI"""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text

email = generate_manager_brief = generate_manager_barrier_brief(
    manager_name="Dr. Priya Mehta",
    team_name="Legal Operations",
    barrier_diagnosis={
        "primary_barrier_category": "confidence",
        "evidence": [
            "31% of the team has an account but only 18% have activated",
            "Survey: 4 of 6 respondents cited 'not sure what to ask it' or 'worried about accuracy for client matters'",
            "No legal-specific prompt examples exist in the shared library"
        ]
    },
    team_metrics={"team_size": 18, "activation_rate": 0.18}
)
print(email)
Barrier Recurrence and Programme Debt
Barriers that are fixed without addressing the underlying system condition will recur with the next cohort of new hires or department expansions. Programme debt accumulates when:
Access barriers are fixed case-by-case
— without updating the IT provisioning SOP, every new hire or role change creates a new access barrier. Fix the process, not just the current cases.
Confidence barriers are addressed with one-off sessions
— without permanent onboarding resources (guided first-session template, safe-to-fail prompt library), each new user re-encounters the same confidence gap. Institutionalise the solution.
Relevance barriers are fixed for one function without creating reusable playbooks
— if a champion spends 10 hours creating bespoke legal prompts but does not add them to the shared library, that work helps only the people they spoke to directly.
Awareness barriers are addressed with campaigns instead of persistent touchpoints

— a one-time email campaign creates a 2-week awareness spike. Embedding Claude mentions into manager onboarding, new-hire orientation, and quarterly all-hands creates durable awareness that does not require repeated campaigns.
Summary

Adoption barrier analysis identifies whether non-adoption stems from access, awareness, confidence, or relevance — four root causes that each require a different intervention. Access barriers are structural and invisible in surveys; they must be ruled out first via provisioning audits. Awareness barriers require targeted comms and champion sessions. Confidence barriers require guided first-use experiences and safe-to-fail environments. Relevance barriers require role-specific playbooks and task-fit demonstrations. Claude can diagnose barriers from funnel data, access audits, and survey text; design structured intervention plans with testable hypotheses; and generate the barrier surveys needed to gather the qualitative data that usage logs cannot provide.
